title_001: My Dealbreaker Button — how easily do you cut people off?
html_003: How easily — or how hard — do you cut people out of your life?
html_004: This measures your inner dealbreaker sensitivity.
html_011: ⚠️ No score, no gauge shows while you’re taking it.
html_012: The button just reacts quietly. Your sensitivity is revealed
html_017: Pick the reaction closest to you.
html_019: Now press the button and reveal your
html_023: Just once — it opens up right now.
html_027: 🌱 Advice for cutting ties in a healthy way
html_030: Screenshot the card and ask a friend: ‘What do you think my dealbreaker sensitivity is?’
html_031: 🧪 Dopamine Lab — see the other tests →
html_032: T H E   M E A N I N G   &   F U N   F A C T O R Y
js_001: A friend cancelled an important plan the day of — for the third time. You?
js_002: I get it, they must have a reason
js_003: Stings a little, but I let it slide
js_004: From now on, I stop expecting anything from them
js_005: I stop making plans with this person at all
js_007: You accidentally find out someone’s been talking about you behind your back. You?
js_008: Ask them directly and clear it up if it’s a mix-up
js_009: Say nothing and slowly put some distance between us
js_010: Let it go once, but bank a quiet warning
js_011: Trust is broken, so I end the relationship
js_012: A close friend has left you on read for months. You?
js_013: Keep checking in — they’ll come around eventually
js_015: Tell them, seriously and once, that it hurt
js_016: Quietly delete them from my contacts
js_017: The date they promised to pay you back is long gone, and they’re acting like the money doesn’t exist. You?
js_018: Figure they’re struggling, and wait a bit longer
js_019: Ask them straight when exactly they’ll pay it back
js_020: Write off the money and the person along with it
js_021: Never see them again, but never bring it up
js_023: In front of a group, you hear a joke that puts you down. You?
js_024: Laugh it off — I don’t want to kill the mood
js_025: Draw a clear line right there and then
js_026: Decide that one more time means we’re done
js_027: Never hang out with that person again
js_029: Someone who’s 30 minutes late every single time. They do apologize each time. You?
js_030: Accept that that’s just who they are
js_031: Tell them straight that my time matters too
js_032: Slowly meet up with them less and less
js_033: Filter out people who can’t keep to a time
js_035: You reached out when you were really struggling, and they brushed you off saying they were busy. You?
js_036: People get busy — I understand and move on
js_037: Tell them honestly that it hurt
js_038: Redefine them as exactly that kind of friend
js_039: Stop sharing my hard times with them, and keep my distance
js_041: You find out they keep telling small but obvious lies. You?
js_042: Anyone could do that — I let it go
js_043: Ask them directly why they did it
js_044: Dock their trust score and quietly watch
js_045: Cut off a liar on the spot
js_047: They keep crossing the line and prying into your private life. You?
js_048: Take it as them caring, and go along with it
js_049: Draw a clear line — say it makes me uncomfortable
js_050: Dodge the topic and manage the distance
js_051: Don’t go deep with people like this — I tidy it up
js_053: You realize they only reach out when they need something. You?
js_054: Still, if I helped, that’s enough
js_055: Next time I’ll ask them for something too, to even it out
js_056: Even when they text, I gradually go lukewarm
js_059: They’re clearly in the wrong, but in the end there’s no apology. You?
js_060: No apology, but I shrug it off
js_062: No apology means I close the door in my heart
js_063: Cut them off without a word
js_065: The group is freezing someone out and wants you to join in. You?
js_066: Go along with the mood and half join in
js_068: Quietly drift away from that group
js_069: Cut off a group like that entirely
js_071: At your big happy news, instead of congrats you catch a jealous reaction. You?
js_072: They’re just bad at showing it, that’s all
js_074: Stop sharing happy news with them from now on
js_075: Tidy up a friendship that can’t truly be happy for me
js_077: Someone you cut off texts you again like nothing ever happened. You?
js_079: Give a chance if there’s an apology and real change
js_083: Without ever checking, they went off a rumor and changed how they treat you. You?
js_084: Wait for time to smooth it over
js_085: Meet them in person and clear up the mix-up
js_086: So that’s how much they trusted me — I keep my distance
js_087: Tidy up people who can’t believe in me
js_089: Somewhere, a click — and it was gone.
js_090: A faint buzz brushed past your fingertip.
js_091: A shadow fell over the button for a moment.
js_093: The needle swayed. Which way, you can’t see.
js_095: The button feels ever so slightly warmer.
js_096: Under the surface, a single gear turned.
js_097: If there’s a number… it’s hidden for now.
js_101: A red light blinked, then faded out.
js_104: Rarely cuts anyone off — the seawall of every relationship
js_105: Your dealbreaker button sits very deep. When someone lets you down, your first move is to understand — ‘they probably had a reason’ — and you carry most wounds quietly. You have the strength to believe in people to the end, but that patience can quietly wear you down.
js_106: You don’t drop people easily, so your relationships last. You stay calm in conflict, and you make the people around you feel safe.
js_107: Enduring becomes a habit, and you keep pushing your own hurt to the back of the line. Your door stays open even to people who treat you carelessly — which burns you out fast.
js_108: Tell ‘understanding’ apart from ‘allowing’. You can understand someone and still not allow the same wound on repeat.
js_109: Before you grit your teeth, ask yourself first: ‘Am I actually okay right now?’ Caring for yourself is part of the relationship too.
js_110: Practice ‘adjusting the distance’ before you jump to cutting off. You can protect yourself without ending things.
js_112: No noise — but you drift away for good
js_113: You don’t fight loud or make declarations. Instead, you start closing the door quietly in your heart. The texts thin out, the warmth cools, and before long that person is outside your radius. It’s clean and drama-free — but the other person can drift away without ever knowing why.
js_114: You tidy up relationships without wasting energy on feelings. You don’t hold grudges, and you know how to protect your own energy.
js_115: Disappearing without a word tends to leave misunderstandings behind. A relationship that could have been repaired can end in silence instead.
js_116: Before you fade out, say what you feel just once. It gives the other person a chance too.
js_117: If you’ve decided to let go, take it as ‘my choice’ instead of beating yourself up.
js_118: Not every relationship has to end in a clean cut. Sometimes ‘a short break’ is an option too.
js_120: Weighs it to the very end before cutting the cord
js_121: You don’t cut people off on feeling alone. You put the situation on a scale, weighing their side against your own hurt, back and forth. You tend to try talking it out first, and only when that leads nowhere do you finally decide. Being careful means fewer regrets — but the slow decisions can drag your heart through it for a long time.
js_122: Because you don’t cut on impulse, your read on a relationship is accurate. You can clear up misunderstandings by talking, so you rarely cut someone off unfairly.
js_123: The deliberating drags on and keeps you hurting far too long. ‘Just one more time’ on repeat can make you miss the moment to decide.
js_124: Set your own ‘cut-off line’ in advance. Once someone crosses it, that’s where you stop agonizing.
js_125: If the scale keeps tipping one way, that’s already your answer. Admit it.
js_126: Spend some of that weighing energy on caring for yourself too. Judgment blurs when your heart is worn out.
js_128: Vanishes underwater instead of facing it
js_129: When conflict shows up, you pick diving over a head-on clash. You don’t fully cut ties — you dodge the messages, put off replies, and wait for things to blur over. It’s your way of keeping the peace, but the problem stays unsolved and the relationship drifts in limbo.
js_130: By avoiding clashes, you leave fewer scars on a relationship. When emotions run high, you know how to step back.
js_131: When avoidance piles up, both the problem and the relationship fester. The other person feels abandoned, and you’re stuck too, unable to reach a conclusion.
js_132: When you want to disappear, tell ‘running away’ apart from ‘a short rest’. If it’s a rest, decide for yourself when you’ll come back.
js_133: Even a quick ‘I can’t talk right now’ helps. It hurts far less than silence.
js_134: Practice saying one honest line head-on, starting with small conflicts. Even when you clash, relationships hold up better than you’d think.
js_136: Gives chances — but the rules are crystal clear
js_137: You’ve got clear rules. A slip earns a warning, a repeat earns a yellow card, and crossing the line means you’re out. You neither endure blindly nor cut in one shot — you manage ‘the number of chances’. It’s fair, but the other person often has no idea you’re counting in your head.
js_138: You manage relationships without getting swept up by emotion. You have a rare balance — giving chances while still protecting yourself.
js_139: When your inner warnings never reach the other person, the cut-off can feel unfair to them. And constantly ‘grading’ people can wear you out.
js_140: Don’t just stack warnings up in your head — say them so the other person can hear. That’s the only way they become a real chance.
js_141: Bend the rules a little person to person. Not every slip weighs the same.
js_142: If you’ve called someone ‘out’, carry it out calmly. The guilt isn’t yours to hold.
js_144: Cross the line once, and it’s over
js_145: Your dealbreaker button is right at your fingertips. One decisive act that breaks your trust, and you won’t look back — no matter how long you’ve known them. Your self-protection is strong and your decisions are fast, but that firmness can sometimes cut off a misunderstanding you could have cleared up.
js_146: You clear out relationships that hurt you, fast. Your self-respect is clear, and you don’t stay stuck in bad relationships for long.
js_147: Judging one slip as beyond repair can cost you good people too. The people close to you may feel the tension of ‘one strike and it’s over’.
js_148: Before you cut, tell ‘a slip’ apart from ‘their nature’, just once. The outcome might change.
js_149: Keep your firmness, but sleep on it for 24 hours first. Decisions made in the rapids are hard to undo.
js_150: Cutting someone off is fair. Just let it be one option among several — not your only card.
js_151: The button sits very, very deep
js_152: The button is right at your fingertips

_app.title: My Dealbreaker Button
_app.desc: How easily do you cut people off? Measure your dealbreaker sensitivity.
